data.title: SLVP: Buy The Dips On This Top-Performing Silver ETF
data.description:
Back in June 2025, I wrote a bullish article about the iShares MSCI Global Silver and Metals Miners ETF (SLVP). I gave it a strong buy rating, and that worked out well because this ETF has soared nearly 60% in the past few months. This solidly beats the nearly 11% gain that the S&P 500 Index (SPY) has generated over the same time period. Since the start of 2025, the returns are even more impressive and SLVP is one of the best-performing ETFs this year, with a gain of about 139% year-to-date. It does not get much better than that, and this ETF has an expense ratio of just 0.39%.

Since it has performed so exceedingly well, I felt this was a good time for an update on this top performer and on silver in general as we go into 2026. With this in mind, let's take a closer look:

The Chart

As the chart below shows, this ETF was trading for just around $12 at the start of 2025, and it is now trading for just over $28. The 50-day moving average is $26.52, and the 200-day moving average is close to $19. These could be levels of strong support, especially the $19 level, but I doubt it will drop to that level, so I would focus on buying pullbacks in the $26 range where the 50-day moving average currently stands.

Overall, this is an impressive chart, but I see one potential red flag, and that is that this ETF recently hit a 52-week high at $31.48, and then there was a pullback. However, it is starting to head higher again, so my concern is that the $31.48 level could be a point of resistance, and there is the possibility that if it does not break past this level, it could become a bearish double top. This is not a major concern yet, just something to watch for in the coming weeks. If SLVP can get past the $31.48 level, then it is obvious the rally is back on and new highs are coming.

Top Ten Holdings

Since my last article, I have noticed that there have been some major changes to the top ten holdings for this ETF. The main difference was that in June, Pan American Silver Corp. (PAAS) represented about 23.53% of the ETF, and now this position is not even in the top ten holdings list. I think this is a positive change to the portfolio because having a single stock representing nearly one-quarter of the fund leads to heightened risk, especially since the mining sector can be volatile due to commodity prices and mining and weather risks.

The current top ten holdings list looks much more balanced, with the largest position weighing in at just over 13% of the portfolio. This portfolio has a mix of silver and gold producers, which could position it for long-term upside with some diversification.

The Outlook For Silver

The outlook remains strong, with Central Banks continuing to load up on gold, which helps boost demand for silver. Concerns about weakness in the U.S. dollar appear poised to continue, especially as the level of U.S. Government debt continues to grow beyond $38 trillion. Fed rate cuts are also likely to boost interest in precious metals and debase the dollar even further. Outside of investor interest in silver, the demand for this precious metal could be strong when it comes to industrial uses. Silver is used in everything from solar panels to electric vehicles to 5G and other emerging technologies.

The Historical Average Gold To Silver Ratio Suggests Significant Upside Potential For Silver

Over the 20th century, the ratio between gold and silver has averaged around 47 to 1. However, right now it takes about 80 ounces of silver to buy 1 ounce of gold. A long time ago, the Roman Empire set the ratio at 12 to 1, and the U.S. Government once set the ratio at 15 to 1 with the Coinage Act of 1792. All of this suggests that silver could have significantly more upside as a potential catch-up trade to gold. While I don't see a 12 to 1 or 15 to 1 ratio likely in modern times, a reversion to the more recent 47 to 1 historical average could be reached, and if so, that suggests a lot more upside for silver.

Let's face it, gold is unreachable for some smaller investors, and even for a gift, a gold coin represents a significant expense, but a one-ounce silver coin or a ten-ounce bar is a more affordable gift or easier-to-make investment regularly when compared to gold. Because of this, I believe a return to a 47-to-1 ratio is possible, although it will possibly require a continued boom in interest from smaller investors over the next few years. Take a look at the chart below, which shows that the gold-to-silver ratio is near historically high levels.

The Potential For A Debt Crisis

The U.S. and the world are awash in debt, and that could lead to a debt crisis. The Federal Reserve Bank of New York recently reported that as of the end of the third quarter of 2025, the total amount of consumer debt reached $18.59 trillion. Furthermore, delinquencies are rising and now represent about 4.5% of consumer loans. The U.S. Government is also carrying massive levels of debt, with the national debt now standing at over $38 trillion. All of this debt appears to be pushing Central Banks and average consumers into wanting to buy and hold hard assets, and this trend into precious metals could accelerate, especially if there is a debt crisis.

The Risks Of SLVP And Silver

Mining stocks are traditionally volatile, and with the huge recent rally in the price of gold and silver, the volatility could increase even further, creating big moves in the price of SLVP. With this ETF up around 100% in 2025, a sharp pullback is increasingly likely. Investing in mining stocks only amplifies the risks of buying physical silver because with mining stocks you have the risk of silver prices falling, but also the geopolitical and regulatory risks. In addition, mining can be impacted by weather, accidents, declining ore grades and more. If there is a recession, this could reduce industrial demand for silver, and that could make it a higher-risk precious metal.

In Summary

I am still bullish on silver and SLVP, but there has already been a huge run, and I expect pullbacks, which I want to take advantage of. Because of this, I believe this ETF is worth holding right now, but I would not buy more until there is a meaningful dip. I believe buying pullbacks in this top-performing ETF will pay off in the long term, since the price of silver appears poised for upside that is driven by excessive consumer and government debt levels and a weakening U.S. dollar. Of course, industrial demand is another major long-term driver for silver prices as well.

data.title: TCW MetWest Low Duration Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review: "Now Showing: A Cooling Labor Market"

If a swift and sharp recovery from tariff-driven volatility was the surprise Spring blockbuster, then a weakening labor market- evidenced by underwhelming payroll prints and significant downward revisions- was the summer flop. Combined with other employment indicators pointing to a low-hiring, low-firing state of the job market, the possibility of a further deterioration prompted the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) to implement a "risk-management cut" with a 25 basis point (bp) reduction to the policy rate. While swaps pricing reflected a unanimous vote by market participants for the rate cut by the time the September meeting arrived, the tenor from within the FOMC over the quarter appeared to be anything but. Indeed, two Governors dissented at the July meeting (the first two-Governor dissent in decades) in favor of an earlier start to rate cuts, while others cautioned against easing in the face of still-elevated inflation. Despite the divide in opinions, preventing any further softening in the labor market was shown to be top priority with the realized rate cut and median FOMC projection of two more cuts by year end. However, there remains lingering uncertainty among some market participants about potential longer-term tariff impacts and the Fed's independence that weighed on longer yields, resulting in their relative underperformance versus the front of the curve. Illustratively, 1- and 2-Year yields fell by 35 and 11 bps, respectively, while the 10- and 30-Year yields moved lower by 8 and 4 bps, respectively.

Despite the potentially troublesome developments that forced the Fed's hand, risk assets turned in another quarter of strong returns as investors appeared to focus more on the valuation impacts of lower rates and less on the underlying reason for the rate cut. Equities once again outperformed with the S&P 500 Index gaining 8% while the tech-heavy NASDAQ Index returned more than 11%.Though more modest, fixed income markets also generated positive returns, with the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index finishing the quarter up 2%. Excluding the rate impact on bonds, the excess return (outperformance versus similar duration Treasuries) for the Aggregate Bond Index reached nearly 50 bps as yield spreads tightened across sectors. The strongest return profile belonged to corporate credit with investment grade and high yield issues gaining 2.6% and 2.5%, respectively, supported by robust demand. With the future path of rates coming into greater clarity over the period, investors sought to lock in corporate yields at still-attractive levels, which also prompted a wave of record supply in both investment grade and high yield markets as borrowers in turn sought to capitalize on receptive capital markets and favorable borrowing costs (falling yields, reduced spreads). Agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) gained 2.4% alongside declining interest rate volatility to lead securitized products, while the non-agency residential MBS sector saw broad-based advances across collateral types as spreads moved tighter and prices increased. Commercial MBS (CMBS) gained 1.8%, with positive total returns from both agency and private label deals, though private label issues outperformed given the risk-on backdrop. Finally, Index-eligible asset-backed securities (ABS) gained 1.6%, though auto and credit card collateral underperformed non-traditional sectors like collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) which benefitted from the strong appetite for spread and yield premiums offered, especially relative to corporates of similar quality and maturity.

Performance

The TCW MetWest Low Duration Bond Fund I-Class (“Fund”) (MWLIX) returned 1.46% net of fees in the third quarter of 2025 to finish ahead of the ICE BofA 1-3 Year U.S. Treasury Index (“Index”) by 34 bps, bringing year-to-date outperformance to 105 bps. For the quarter, the Fund benefitted from allocations across securitized sectors, including the large allocation to agency MBS given the positive performance at the sector level amid easing rate volatility. Non-agency MBS also contributed as spreads tightened and prices rose across both legacy and more recent vintage issues. Away from residential MBS, private label CMBS holdings were additive, led by single asset single borrower deal structures that have performed well year-to-

date in the broader risk-on backdrop and continued sponsorship of performing properties. Meanwhile, tighter spreads and sustained demand were prevalent in the ABS sector as well, with the Fund's non-traditional collateral types like data centers, single-family rentals, and CLOs driving the largest contributions. Moving to corporate credit, the sizeable allocation to investment grade issues rewarded returns as the cohort bested Treasuries during the period, with the emphasis on large U.S. banks bolstering performance as they were among the best performers in the short corporate universe. Finally, the Fund's overweight duration position and emphasis on the front-end of the curve was modestly positive as the curve steepened during the quarter.

Outlook & Positioning

We entered the year with a recognition that the distribution of possible economic outcomes had become wider given the administration's penchant for volatility, with a bias towards more pessimistic possibilities. While the view remains that the economy is likely to slow from here, it has become increasingly clear that policy sequencing matters, and that the administration's more aggressive policies and attendant headwinds appear to be fading. In contrast, there are components of the administration's agenda that are pro-growth and that are likely to provide economic tailwinds in the future, but the ability of the economy to bridge the gap between then and now is made more complicated by the demonstrable weakening that has occurred in the labor market. This development in our view is the single-largest determinant for the future direction of the economy; the jobs market turning from “low-hiring, low-firing” to "no-hiring and firing" likely means the difference between a mere slowing of growth and something that more closely resembles a recession, while the speed with which the pro-growth policy can be implemented and provide economic support is also set to play a meaningful yet protracted part. Regardless of which path the economy ultimately travels, pricing across most risk assets appears to be focused on only the most optimistic of outcomes as equity markets continue to reach new highs and credit spreads remain near historical lows, providing investors minimal protection against future volatility and necessitating diligent underwriting.

As value investors, TCW remains vigilant in its assessment of risks and opportunities across markets. With current corporate spread levels presenting an asymmetric risk and reward profile, the allocation to corporate credit remains cautious, with selective exposure that is more defensive in nature (up in quality, emphasis on liquidity, minimal cyclical exposures, focus on high-quality banks). Securitized products, however, currently offer a more compelling relative value argument and thus represent a more attractive segment of the market in which to allocate portfolio risk budgets. Agency MBS represents a significant position given the sector's liquidity, spread premiums, and government guarantee, with exposure focused on belly coupons and floating rate CMOs for incremental income. Non-agency MBS allocation is favored for robust collateral profiles that allow for various opportunities to capture strong risk-adjusted yields. Meanwhile, CMBS exposure is focused on single asset single borrower deals, selected based on a detailed analysis of the underlying property and sponsor. ABS positions are biased toward AAA CLOs and high-quality non-traditional collateral types like student loans. Finally, expectations for rates to continue moving lower and for the Fed to move more quickly than anticipated by markets should the labor market weaken further informs a still longer-than-index duration position of approximately 0.3 years, and an overweight to the front-end of the curve

With respect to non-agency MBS, there are no direct or indirect government or agency guarantees of payments in pools created by non-governmental issuers. GLOSSARY OF TERMS

Agency MBS-The purchase of mortgage-backed securities issued by government-sponsored enterprises such as Ginnie Mae, Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. Asset-Backed Securities - A financial security backed by a loan, lease or receivables against assets other than real estate and mortgage-backed securities. Basis Point (bps)- One hundredth of one percent, used chiefly in expressing differences of interest rates. Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index or “the Agg” - Is a broad-based fixed-income index used by bond traders and the managers of mutual funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) as a benchmark to measure their relative performance. Bond - A fixed income instrument that represents a loan made by an investor to a borrower (typically corporate or governmental). A bond could be thought of as an I.O.U. between the lender and borrower that includes the details of the loan and its payments. A bond has an end date when the principal of the loan is due to be paid to the bond owner and usually includes the terms for variable or fixed interest payments that will be made by the borrower. Bonds are used by companies, municipalities, states, and sovereign governments to finance projects and operations. Owners of bonds are debtholders, or creditors, of the issuer. Call -To force an option writer to sell shares of stock at a price stipulated in a contract. Capital Structure - A capital structure is a mix of a company's long-term debt, specific short-term debt, common equity and preferred equity. The capital structure is how a firm finances its overall operations and growth by using different sources of funds. Carry Trade - A strategy in which an investor sells a certain currency with a relatively low interest rate and uses the funds to purchase a different currency yielding a higher interest rate. CMBS (Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities) - A debt obligation that represents claims to the cash flows from pools of mortgage loans on commercial property. CLO (Collateralized Loan Obligations) - A special purpose vehicle (SPV) with securitization payments in the form of different tranches. Financial institutions back this security with receivables from loans. Collateral - Property or other assets that a borrower offers a lender to secure a loan. Corporate- Of or relating to a bond issued by a corporation as opposed to a bond issued by the U.S. Treasury, a non-U.S. government or a municipality. Corporate Credit - A term that is used in written investment materials and commentaries to refer to a corporation's debt or to the corporate debt market as a whole. Coupon - The interest rate stated on a bond when it's issued. The coupon is typically paid semiannually. Duration - A measure of the sensitivity of the price (the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. Duration is expressed as a number of years. Rising interest rates mean falling bond prices, while declining interest rates mean rising bond prices. Excess Return- The rate of return on a security or an investment portfolio in excess of the return generated by a portfolio of U.S. Treasury securities with similar duration and partial durations. Federal Reserve (the Fed)- The central bank of the United States which regulates the U.S. monetary and financial system. Floating Rate-Any interest rate that changes on a periodic basis. The change is usually tied to movement of an outside indicator, such as the prime interest rate. FOMC (Federal Open Market Committee) - The branch of the Federal Reserve Board that determines the direction of monetary policy. Geopolitical -relating to politics, especially international relations, as influenced by geographical factors. Headwinds - Headwinds in an economic situation represent events or conditions e.g. a credit crisis, rising costs, natural disasters, etc, that slow down the growth of an economy. High Yield - A bond that is rated below investment grade. Inflation - A condition of a rise in the general level of prices of goods and services in an economy over a period of time. Investment Grade - A bond that is rated Baa3/BBB- or higher by Moody's, Standard & Poor's and Fitch. Labor Market - The nominal market in which workers find paying work, employers find willing workers, and wage rates are determined. Liquidity - The ability to convert an asset to cash quickly. High Yield - A bond that is rated below investment grade. Nasdaq- Nasdaq is a global electronic marketplace for buying and selling securities, as well as the benchmark index for U.S. technology stocks. Nasdaq was created by the National Association of Securities Dealers (NASD) to enable investors to trade securities on a computerized, speedy and transparent system, and commenced operations on February 8, 1971.The term “Nasdaq” is also used to refer to the Nasdaq Composite, an index of more than 3,000 stocks listed on the Nasdaq exchange that includes the world's foremost technology and biotech giants such as Apple, Google, Microsoft, Oracle, Amazon, Intel and Amgen. Non-agency - Mortgage backed securities made up of mortgage loans that are not guaranteed by a government-supported agency. Non-Agency MBS - Mortgage backed securities sponsored by private companies other than government sponsored enterprises such as Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. Non-cyclical - Non-cyclical stocks repeatedly outperform the market when economic growth slows. Non-cyclical securities are generally profitable regardless of economic trends because they produce or distribute goods and services we always need, including things like food, power, water, and gas. Overweight-A condition where the portfolio exposure to a given asset class(or risk measure) exceeds that of the benchmark index. Recession - Two consecutive quarters of negative economic growth as measured by a country's gross domestic product. Risk Asset- Any asset that carries a degree of risk. Risk Premium (or Yield Premium)- The minimum rate of return demanded by holders of a risky asset in excess of the return on a risk-free asset with similar maturity and duration profile. S&P 500 - The S&P 500 Index, or Standard & Poor's 500 Index, is a market-capitalization-weighted index of 500 leading publicly traded companies in the U.S. The index actually has 503 components because three of them have two share classes listed. Securitized - When a bank pools together different kinds of loans to clear them off its balance sheet, to free up credit for new lenders, and to create new securities that can be marketed and sold to investors. Spread - The difference between the bid and the ask price of a security or asset. Swaps - The most common and simplest swap, an agreement between two parties to exchange sequences of cash flows for a set period of time. Tariff - A tax imposed on imported goods and services. Total Return - The rate of return on a security, including income from dividends and interest, as well as appreciation or depreciation in the price of the security, over a given time period of time. Tranches - Are segments created from a pool of securities -usually debt instruments such as bonds or mortgages-that are divided up by risk, time to maturity, or other characteristics in order to be marketable to different investors. Each portion or tranche of a securitized or structured product is one of several related securities offered at the same time, but with varying risks, rewards and maturities to appeal to a diverse range of investors. Underweight-A condition where a portfolio does not hold a sufficient amount of a particular security when compared to the security's weight in the underlying benchmark portfolio. U.S. Treasuries (U.S. Treasury Securities) - Bills, notes and bonds that are debt obligations of the U.S. government. Valuations - The process of determining the current worth of an asset or company. There are many techniques that can be used to determine value, some are subjective and others are objective. Volatility - A measure of the risk of price moves for a security calculated from the standard deviation of day to day logarithmic historical price changes. Yield - The income return on an investment. This refers to the interest or dividends received from a security and is usually expressed annually as a percentage based on the investment's cost, its current market value or its face value. Yield Curve-A curve on a graph in which the yield of fixed-interest securities is plotted against the length of time they have to run to maturity. Yield Premium- Additional return.

TCW MetWest Funds are distributed by TCW Funds Distributors LLC.

The TCW MetWest Funds are advised by Metropolitan West Asset Management, LLC, which is a wholly-owned subsidiary of The TCW Group, Inc.

data.title: IREN: Microsoft Deal Is A Game-Changer (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
GPU-as-a-Service cloud computing platform IREN (IREN) delivered a strong earnings report card for the first fiscal quarter last week that showed material momentum in the company's top line as well as a guideline for a massive GPU deployment in the next 5 quarters. IREN is growing its top line at triple digits (+355% Y/Y) and seeing surging profitability… all of which points to a successively expanding run-rate in its annual recurring revenue business.

The Cloud platform also just announced a transformative, multi-billion-dollar Cloud deal with Microsoft (MSFT), a major hyperscaler that also made similar deals with CoreWeave (CRWV) and Nebius (NBIS). This deal will accelerate the firm's Data Center-driven growth and help the company transition to its AI Cloud Service business model. I like the setup for IREN and see the Microsoft deal as a game-changer for IREN.

Previous Rating

IREN submitted a strong outlook about the number of Blackwell GPUs (~60,000) it wanted to deploy in order to tap into the booming GPU-as-a-Service Cloud computing market... leading me to initiate the Cloud player as a buy in early September: Major Inflection Point.

Accelerating CapEx spending and the massive demand represented by hyperscalers for GPU compute continue to be two main growth drivers for the start-up. The company signed a massive deal with Microsoft in early November, which will lift its revenue up by an order of magnitude. I like IREN more after the Microsoft agreement and see a path for an accelerating growth runway; hence my upgrade to strong buy.

Transformative Microsoft Deal Leads To Rating Upgrade

The Cloud computing platform reported better-than-expected revenues for the first fiscal quarter: IREN's revenues surged, on the back of red-hot, enterprise-driven demand for GPUs, to $240.3M in Q1'26, beating the consensus estimate of $228.5M by a good margin. At the same time, the company is seeing surging profitability in major key metrics such as adjusted EBITDA, which went up ~3,600% year-over-year to $91.7M.

The majority of the firm's revenue still came from Bitcoin mining activity, a business that the company has said it will pause in order to make a strategic pivot into the GPU-as-a-Service market, which is rapidly growing. The GPU-as-a-Service market is obviously propelled by higher CapEx spending related to progress in the AI realm, and Precedence Research projects that the market is set for a 6.4X factor increase between FY 2025 and FY 2034, at which point the market is set to be worth $31.9B.

This market expansion is set to benefit the company immensely. IREN has guided an accelerating shift towards AI Cloud Services and the recent Microsoft deal already indicates that the Cloud platform is set for a major revenue boost. In Q1'26, a massive 97% of revenue came from the firm's cryptocurrency mining activities, but over time the revenue mix will change in favor of Cloud Services. The firm benefited here from growing operational mining capacity as well as favorable market conditions in the cryptocurrency market.

The company has guided for the first major phase of its transition to AI Cloud Services to be completed by the end of next year (2026), which is when IREN expects to have deployed the majority of its GPUs (discussed further below). Since the Cloud company is still very much in the process of shifting its business over to GPU deployment, IREN has considerable execution risk in the short term.

On the other hand, should the pivot to AI Cloud be successful, and investors gain more revenue visibility, confidence in the platform's execution could trigger a revaluation of IREN's shares and also improve the quality of the platform's revenue mix long term.

IREN's AI Cloud Service still represents only a small fraction of total revenues (~3.0% in Q1'26), but as the company transitions to large-scale GPU deployment next year, the percentage of AI Cloud Service revenue is set to rise drastically. Additionally, the 5-year duration of the Microsoft deal should also improve revenue predictability (and limit investment risks for investors), which in turn should make it easier for them to project the firm's revenues going forward.

Related to this, because Bitcoin mining is inherently volatile as it depends on factors like the Bitcoin price, hash rates, and electricity costs, IREN's gross profits and EBITDA have been volatile over time.

About 96% of IREN's gross profit in the September quarter (86% in Q1'25) came from Bitcoin mining, so investors will see a deterioration in profitability as the firm pivots its business model. However, due to the significance of the Microsoft Cloud contract, IREN has a strong chance to benefit from higher and more stable earnings in the future if this transition goes according to plan.

IREN is already seeing strong business momentum, as one would expect, given the recent flurry of AI deals in the Data Center market. Nebius signed a major $17.4B GPU compute deal with Microsoft in September, while CoreWeave signed a follow-on $6.5B deal with OpenAI (OPENAI), also in September. Both deals are worth billions of dollars and highlight the severity of the current GPU supply shortage in the market.

Nvidia (NVDA) itself signed a $100B deal with OpenAI which goes to show how much money is currently flushing around the Data Center industry. While IREN is an evolving player in the market for GPU-as-a-Service offers, its business is on fire. It is not growing as fast as either CoreWeave or Nebius, which have already established Data Center platforms, but the firm's growth is pretty impressive nonetheless...

Going forward, I see CoreWeave, Nebius, and IREN as potential winners from a long runway in renting out their high-performance GPUs to enterprise clients and hyperscalers, and more deals with companies like OpenAI, Microsoft, and Meta (META) could soon be announced.

IREN is rapidly building out its GPU footprint and expanding its capacity... which is where additional upside in the company's annual recurring revenue business could come from. In my last work I detailed the company's platform business, which mainly consists of Data Centers in Canada, British Columbia (Childress), and Texas (Sweetwater) with a total operating capacity of 810 megawatts.

The company's Sweetwater sites in Texas are going through a serious upgrade right now, with IREN targeting to bring its Data Center capacity to 2,000 megawatts in the next two years: Sweetwater 1 will add 1,400 megawatts in FY 2026, while Sweetwater 2 is scheduled to deliver 600 megawatts in late FY 2027.

The Data Center platform announced a transformative deal with Microsoft in early November that is set to lift the firm's revenues into a new league. The Microsoft deal, which was announced only days before IREN reported results for its first fiscal quarter, has a contract value of $9.7B, a five-year deal term, and includes a 20% upfront prepayment.

The company is currently on a ~$1.0B quarterly annualized revenue run-rate, but the deal is set to push the firm's annual recurring revenue size to $3.4B by next year, based off of a then deployed GPU count of 140k units... showing a more than 6.0X factor increase over the end of Q1'26.

Catalysts

There are a number of catalysts for IREN that I feel support a rating upgrade:

  • CapEx spending in the Data Center market is accelerating
  • As IREN is a Preferred Nvidia partner, the company is set to get access to the firm's next-gen Data Center GPU, the Vera Rubin (to be released next year), which could make IREN more appealing to hyperscalers going forward
  • New hyperscalers could be attracted to the company's GPU platform following the transformative Microsoft deal. My guess is that especially Meta could potentially sign a GPU compute deal here
  • As the company transitions its business model to AI Cloud services, IREN's revenue volatility should decrease while its earnings visibility should improve (GPU rental contracts are typically multi-year contracts) going forward.

IREN's Valuation

IREN was profitable in Q1'26, but many of the company's Data Center rival platforms are not, so I believe the best approach to valuing the company is by using a P/S ratio.

The Cloud computing platform is currently valued at a forward price-to-revenue ratio of 6.8X, which is not outrageous considering that IREN is expected to grow its revenues 100% this year and 157% next year. Further, IREN has a very small market valuation ($17.7B) relative to its larger rivals, leaving a lot more room for market cap growth.

The industry group I am using here, includes IREN, Nebius, and CoreWeave, as all companies run GPU-as-a-Service models and are in close partnership with the world’s leading AI GPU company, Nvidia. IREN is a Preferred Partner for Nvidia, and so are the other two, meaning all companies have priority GPU access. The industry group average P/S ratio is 9.2X and highlights that IREN may be undervalued, especially in the context of a major revenue and ARR ramp next year.

The revenue trend for IREN is highly favorable as well, with the Microsoft deal making a big impact on the company's revenue estimates. As the company positions itself to serve top-tier hyperscalers, like Microsoft, IREN could strike similar deals with other Data Center infrastructure companies. An industry group average P/S ratio of 9.2X implies a fair value of ~$85 for IREN's shares, which would leave ~36% upside on the table.

Risks With IREN

IREN has a number of risks. The Data Center platform is not as established as CoreWeave or Nebius and therefore has considerable execution risks. IREN has a relatively small market cap, which highlights that the company is still a relatively small Cloud player and still needs to convince investors that it can pull off a strategic pivot into the GPU-as-a-Service market for hyperscalers. It also still depends to a large extent on Bitcoin mining, but as the company shifts its business model over to Cloud services, the revenue mix is going to change, which should lead to less volatility in revenues and earnings. If this business pivot proves unsuccessful, IREN may be subjected to a serious valuation drawdown.

Closing Thoughts

IREN's business is performing really well, and the company is continuing to see triple-digit top-line momentum amid surging demand from hyperscalers for new GPU capacity in order to run highly demanding AI workloads. The company is in the process of ramping up its GPU deployment and is targeting ~140,000 GPUs in service by the end of the next year. The Microsoft deal is game-changing and highly transformative for IREN because it not only fuels the firm's ARR business, but it also accelerates the firm's transition to becoming an AI Cloud Services provider. I like IREN also because of its strong EBITDA trend, strong focus on data center expansion, as well as low market cap... which leaves a ton of room for growth.

data.title: WhiteHorse Finance: Losses Likely To Continue Following Q3 Earnings
data.description:
Overview

WhiteHorse Finance (WHF) operates as a business development company that aims to provide attractive total returns from its portfolio of debt investments. Unlike traditional equities, these debt investments don't rely on the earnings growth of their positions but instead on borrowers' ability to repay their debt. When I previously covered WHF, I issued a sell rating due to the declining earnings and weak dividend coverage. WHF recently reported its Q3 earnings, so I wanted to revisit the BDC's performance and outlook for the remainder of the year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that WHF's share price has declined by nearly 34.5%. WHF has essentially failed to participate in the market rally over the last few months. Even when including all distributions that were paid, the total return still sits at a loss of 22.2% over the same period. WHF now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 14.9%, which can be enticing for investors seeking a high level of income generation. However, I maintain caution about the overall sustainability of payouts going forward.

Due to the continued share price decline, WHF now trades at one of the largest discounts to NAV valuations over the last five years. Despite this, I do not believe that this justifies a buy, as I believe that the share price can continue its decline. After reviewing the latest earnings, WHF continues to demonstrate issues that can cause its NAV to decline. For instance, management has not allocated a meaningful level of capital towards new investment activity and its base assets have not increased. So let's start by assessing the latest earnings report.

Q3 Earnings

According to the latest portfolio overview, WHF has investments at a fair value of $568.4M spread across 66 different portfolio companies. WHF's portfolio of investments is relatively small, with an average investment size of $3.8M with a weighted average yield on income-producing debt investments of 11.6%. In order to generate its income, WHF leans heavily on first lien senior secured debt investments. This is ideal because first lien debt sits at the top of the corporate capital structure, which has the highest priority for repayment. The different debt types WHF utilizes are as follows:

  • First Lien: 75%.
  • STRS Joint Venture: 9%.
  • Equity: 6%.
  • Second Lien: 1%.

Expanding on this diversity, WHF maintains an efficient level of diversity across different industries to limit concentration risk. The only challenge is that WHF is exposed to industries that may be impacted by the uncertainty of tariffs. For instance, WHF is most heavily allocated to Air Freight & Logistics investments at 6.3% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Integrated Telecommunication Services and Data Processing investments, which both account for 5.9% of assets, respectively.

WHF recently reported its Q3 earnings this week and the results were disappointing. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.26 per share, which missed expectations by $0.03. This also represents a large decline from the prior year's net investment income of $0.39 per share. WHF has seen its net investment income decline for four consecutive quarters now. Additionally, net realized losses were quite large at $0.28 per share, resulting in a net decrease in net assets of ~$600K.

On a more positive note, WHF did see its total operating expenses slightly decline over the quarter, which now amounted to $11.6M and was a decline from the prior year's operating expenses of $13.7M. This can be attributed to a decline in total interest expenses and performance-based incentive fees. However, base management fees have remained the same at $2.8M.

From a balance sheet perspective, WHF has also seen its net assets decline over the last quarter to $626M, most attributed to a decline in the fair value of its investments. WHF now holds $45.9M in cash and equivalents, which does represent an increase from the prior quarter's balance of $33.3M. Additionally, WHF's total debt has declined to $323.5M, which helped reduce total liabilities down to $360.8M. The reduction in debt has helped WHF reduce its net leverage ratio down to 1.07x.

WHF has a staggered debt maturity with the majority of its debt maturing in 2037. However, WHF does have $85M in debt outstanding that will mature over the course of 2026. Since WHF has improved its balance sheet in terms of cash and debt, there should be no issues navigating these maturities.

Valuation & Risk Profile

Since WHF is structured as a business development company, the price can trade at a difference from the underlying value of its assets. Due to the continuous declines, WHF now trades at a discount to NAV of 39.18%, which is near the largest discounted price to NAV over the last five years. For reference, WHF has traded at an average discount to NAV of 10.07% over the last five-year period. However, this discount is a result of the performance weakness and I don't think that this is a good time to accumulate shares.

Wall Street analysts currently have an average price target of $7.67 per share, which indicates a potential upside of about 11.4% from its current level. However, I believe that this double-digit growth rate is very unlikely considering management's failure to allocate a meaningful level of capital to new investments. For instance, management was able to achieve $21.9M in gross fundings over the quarter. However, this was offset by $76.8M of exits and repayments, resulting in a net decline of $54.9M for the quarter. As a result, the NAV has continued its decline for over a year now. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • Q3 2024: $12.77 per share.
  • Q4 2024: $12.31 per share.
  • Q1 2025: $12.11 per share.
  • Q2 2025: $11.82 per share.
  • Q3 2025: $11.41 per share.

From a risk perspective, WHF has made some improvements in its non-accrual investments. Non-accruals represent portfolio companies that are materially underperforming and cannot keep up with their required debt maintenance. On the latest earnings call, WHF confirmed that non-accruals have improved to 2.7% of fair portfolio value.

Nonaccrual investments now represent 2.7% of the debt portfolio at fair value, an improvement compared with 4.9% of the debt portfolio in the prior quarter. Other deals on nonaccrual are likely to remain that way for some period of time. We are continuing to actively work on getting deals off nonaccrual, leveraging the expertise of our 5-person dedicated WhiteHorse restructuring team and the resources of H.I.G. Capital. Aside from the credits on nonaccrual, our portfolio is performing quite well. - Stuart Aronson, CEO.

While it's great that non-accruals have slightly improved, I believe that the greater risk comes from the inability to continue growing its portfolio. Although the Fed recently initiated its second 25 basis point cut to interest rates, the federal funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. As the cost of debt remains high, I believe there will be limited growth opportunities for management to allocate capital to in the near term. Therefore, losses can continue since there is no indication that the NAV deterioration will stop over the next quarter.

Thin Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.25 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 14.9%. As previously mentioned, net investment income amounted to $0.26 per share for the quarter. This indicates that earnings currently support the distribution at a tight coverage rate of 104%. Since the NAV has continued to decline and new investments are unlikely to offset the lower net investment income levels, I believe that there's the potential for an additional dividend cut to take place over the next twelve months. For this reason, it's difficult to recommend a position in WHF for income investors seeking stability.

Management also did not confirm any specific details about whether or not there was any undistributed spillover income that could be tapped into. In a scenario where interest rates continue to trend downward over the next twelve months, there's a chance that net investment income will decline and threaten the current sustainability of payouts. Since 99.1% of the investments are structured on a floating rate basis, we can expect that a lower interest rate environment would directly translate to a lower level of interest income that can be collected from their borrowers.

Lastly, it's worth mentioning that the distributions received from WHF offer no tax efficiency. Since distributions are fueled by the repayment of loans, the income received would be classified as ordinary dividends, which have the highest tax burden for investors. So if you're an investor who wants to limit the overall tax liability, a position in WHF would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my sell rating on WHF at this time due to the continued NAV deterioration, lower earnings, and thin dividend coverage. Although the underlying rate of non-accruals has increased, the portfolio's fair value and growth potential have decreased. Management has failed to allocate a meaningful level of capital towards new investment activity, which severely limits growth potential in the near term. Assuming that interest rates continue to trend downward, there's also a chance that net investment income would decline, which puts the dividend under threat of another reduction. For these reasons, I will remain on the sidelines and refrain from initiating a position, despite the large discount to NAV valuation.

data.title: Brookfield: Q3's Dip Is My Early Christmas Gift
data.description:
It wasn't long ago that I initiated my position in Brookfield Corporation (BN). In fact, it was shortly after my analysis “Brookfield: A Generational Opportunity To Compound Wealth.” Interestingly, in the meantime, BN stock has corrected -8% and is now trading at $43.

I don't usually start with large positions, preferring to buy gradually, so I consider this dip a great gift, mainly because I believe Brookfield's long-term case is intact, and in fact, I really liked Q3, unlike the market.

Recalling Brookfield's Case

Recalling a little of the case, for those who did not read my last analysis, I consider this to be a “generational opportunity” company precisely because of compounding. The asset management business is excellent, with a robust track record, which also encompasses the operating business, since the company has already proven its discipline in allocating this capital very well and riding trends such as power solutions, data centers, and infrastructure in general.

This is further supported by an insurance business that, in addition to being good, also has very positive estimates, such as reaching $350 billion in insurance assets in 2030 due to synergies, organic inflows, and M&A.

All these trends, moats, and a proven management vision based on history lead me to believe that distributable earnings (which I will refer to as DE from now on) could actually have a CAGR in the high teens until 2030.

In addition to Q3, one of the news items that has come out since my last rating on Brookfield stock was that the company will acquire the remaining interest in Oaktree. Howard Marks himself, an investor I personally admire greatly, stated about this acquisition, and this sums up well why I also understand that this is a move that only corroborates the robustness of Brookfield's ecosystem:

“Our partnership with Brookfield has been a great success, built on shared values of disciplined investing, long-term thinking, and integrity. Together, we’ve proven our ability to work seamlessly and deliver the best of both firms to our clients. Becoming part of Brookfield in full is a natural evolution that will allow Oaktree to continue thriving as part of one of the world’s leading investment organizations. With this closer alignment, Oaktree will remain central to Brookfield’s credit strategy, and we see significant opportunities to grow the franchise and expand what we can offer our clients together.”

Brookfield Had A Great Quarter

It's hard to say exactly why BN stock dipped after Q3 earnings, since the main metric, DE, delivered $0.63 per share, beating the $0.60 consensus. But I have some suspicions as to why. The first is that asset management DE fell about -1% YoY, and at first glance, this seems very bad since the business is very reliable. But still, note in the table below how the other divisions grew more healthily. Wealth Solutions grew 15% YoY, and operating businesses grew 3% YoY.

But even though this is the main KPI to analyze in Brookfield's case, we have to take a step back and look at the operational side first.

In the case of the Asset Management business, fee-related earnings grew 17% YoY, reaching a quarterly record. Total inflows were $380 billion, another record if we consider the 3-year period. In other words, this lower DE is not directly related to the core business, but rather to other factors such as lower gains on realizations, in addition to other pressures on non-recurring net income.

If we look at this quarter's income statement, we see that revenues decreased slightly, but in addition, income tax was -$504 million, while in Q3 2024 it was -$138 million. In addition, fair value changes had a negative impact of -$736 million.

In other words, I do not believe that the Asset Management business performed poorly; on the contrary, it was a very robust result, which only strengthens my conviction that this is a compounding business.

As for other operational highlights, Wealth Solutions originated $5 billion in annuity sales in the quarter, reaching $139 billion in insurance assets, in addition to deploying $4 billion in Brookfield's own strategies, showing how these businesses are complementary. In addition, the company obtained approval for the acquisition of Just Group, which is another trigger that will increase total insurance assets to ~$180 billion, possibly in 2026.

In Operating Businesses, the company announced some initiatives related to next-gen power and AI, such as the partnership with the U.S. Government to increase nuclear power capacity through Westinghouse, delivering about $80 billion in new nuclear plants.

Returning to trying to explain the dip, it may also have to do with macroeconomic conditions, since Brookfield's business is directly related to interest rates, and even though there are some positive signs, the current environment is still one of high-interest rates, high government debt, and so on. But I also view this with a certain optimism, as mentioned in the earnings call. If/when this changes, it could be very beneficial for Brookfield's industry, and I believe it could also trigger a re-rating of the stock:

“if growth stays modest, policymakers may instead quietly manage rates below inflation to ease debt burdens, lowering short rates and guiding long rates down. If this path is pursued, it would likely lead to a period of declining real yields and low nominal rates. This environment will provide the optimal conditions for real assets we invest into. Our portfolio is built around inflation-linked durable cash flows backed by hard assets that protect real returns. “

Everything I mentioned above only strengthens my conviction in compounding. The Asset Management business is doing extremely well, very complementary to Wealth Solutions, and the operating businesses, even though they are not growing at the same pace as these two, I also see a lot of value with all these secular trends, such as nuclear energy, aging population, and AI.

Brookfield’s Valuation

I still think Brookfield’s current price allows (and very much so) for compounding. We are talking about a DE in the last quarter of $0.63 per share, which annualized comes to $2.52. After the dip, we are talking about a price-to-DE of ~17x, just annualizing what has already been delivered, without considering all the growth.

Now consider that this DE should grow in double digits, in my view, not only until 2030, but possibly until 2040, since many of these opportunities will still exist in the next decade, as management itself suggests when referring to them as “multi-decade.”

Today, reversing the multiple (2.52/43), we are talking about an implied yield of 5.8%, already with a relevant premium over 10-year Treasury Yields, and this is to gain exposure to a company with moats, an excellent track record, and plenty of opportunities for growth.

Compared to its peers, BN stock also stands out, as in price-to-book, which is at 2.48x, while peers such as KKR & Co. (KKR) and Apollo Global Management (APO) trade between 3.5x and 4x.

The Bottom Line

In short, I believe that Brookfield's latest quarter and news only support the optimistic long-term outlook. Amidst all the AI hype, having such a solid company in the portfolio, with a strong real assets division, is very valuable. As I am still building my position, this dip is very welcome, and I will use it to buy more, as I will likely do with the next ones if there are any.

Obviously, there are risks involved, but they are mainly related to execution. For example, even though the company has many opportunities and invests billions in data centers and nuclear energy, there is no guarantee that these investments will yield high returns. Basically, we have to rely on our trust in Brookfield's team. In addition, these investments may be affected by external factors, such as competition and interest rates, as I mentioned throughout the article.

That said, Brookfield is still one of my favorite companies to buy today. Therefore, I am upgrading my rating to strong buy.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Merck Talks Send Cidara Soaring
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Merck (MRK) seen nearing acquisition of Cidara Therapeutics (CDTX) in deal potentially above $3.3 billion. (00:23) U.S., Argentina to open markets to key products in trade framework. (01:08) DoorDash (DASH) reveals October cybersecurity breach impacting its platform. (02:04)

This is an abridged transcript.

Cidara Therapeutics (CDTX) is up 85% as of the time of this recording.

This follows news that Merck (MRK) is nearing a deal to buy the company.

The Financial Times reported that Cidara is a drugmaker pioneering a long-acting antibody medicine that protects against flu.

A deal valuing Cidara at a premium to its $3.3 billion market capitalization could be announced as soon as Friday.

Merck was still vying with another pharmaceutical group for the biotech late on Thursday before the seller favoured its offer.

The exact price of the deal could not immediately be established.

According to the FT, any deal would likely involve cash upfront along with additional payouts tied to future clinical trial milestones.

The United States said Thursday it will lift tariffs on certain foods and other imports from Argentina, Ecuador, Guatemala, and El Salvador as part of new framework agreements aimed at expanding market access for U.S. companies.

Under the agreement, Argentina will offer preferential access for a variety of U.S. exports, including medicines, chemicals, machinery, IT products, medical devices, motor vehicles, and numerous agricultural goods.

Ecuador will lower or eliminate several tariff barriers on key products such as tree nuts, fresh fruit, pulses, wheat, wine, and distilled spirits. It will also completely remove a variable tariff on many agricultural products previously imposed under the Andean Price Band System.

The White House says in the coming weeks the countries will work on finalizing the deal so the leaders can put pen to paper.

More on the agreements in the Wall Street Breakfast newsletter. A link to sign up is in the show notes section.

DoorDash (DASH) said that its team recently identified and shut down a cybersecurity incident that involved an unauthorized third-party gaining access to and taking certain user information.

In a post on its website, the company said there is no indication that data was misused for fraud, identity theft.

DoorDash added that it’s boosting its security measures, rolling out more training for employees, bringing in an outside firm to help with the investigation, and bringing in law enforcement.

This isn’t the first time the company has dealt with a breach — back in 2019, information from about 5 million customers, Dashers, and merchants was exposed to an unauthorized party.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

Fed's Kashkari did not back last rate cut, undecided on December - report

Google proposes changes to adtech business after EU fine, but no asset breakup

Blue Origin launches NASA's Mars mission, sticks booster landing for the first time

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is up 2% at $60/barrel. Bitcoin is down 2.6% at $97,000. Gold is flat at $4,169.

The FTSE 100 is down 1.2% and the DAX is down 0.8%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Virgin Galactic (SPCE) +4% - Shares rose after the company beat top- and bottom-line estimates and narrowed its quarterly loss through lower operating expenses

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 3:20 pm Atlanta Federal Reserve Bank President Raphael Bostic will participate in moderated conversation before the Association for Public Policy Analysis and Management annual conference.

And since it's Friday you know that means it’s Seeking Alpha weekly news quiz day. I’ll leave a link in the show notes section.

data.title: Guggenheim Macro Opportunities Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

  • The Fund returned 2.08 percent in the third quarter, outperforming its benchmark, the ICE Bank of America 3-Month U.S. Treasury Bill Index, by 1.0 percent.
  • Positive performance was driven primarily by carry (earned income).
  • Spread tightening within high-yield (HY) and investment-grade (IG) corporates drove positive spread performance as risk-on market sentiment led to broad tightening of credit spreads during the quarter.
  • Duration’s impact on performance was mostly neutral.

Strategy and Positioning

  • The Fund modestly reduced exposure to corporate credit, instead favoring mortgage credit sectors like Agency and non-Agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS), reflecting improved relative value vs. corporate bonds and stable underlying fundamentals.
  • Duration positioning remained mostly unchanged over the quarter at roughly 2.4 years, near the long-term average level for the Fund, and focused on front/intermediate tenors.
  • The Fund increased allocations to certain structured credit sectors that can offer attractive risk-adjusted yields, supporting strong carry income and offering total return potential if spreads tighten.
  • The Fund continued to increase exposure to eurozone credit, reflecting improved relative value in non-U.S. sectors, especially within senior loans.

QTD Contributors / Detractors

  • Carry contributed 1.5 percent to return while duration contribution was flat.
  • Spread tightening across IG and HY corporates were the primary contributors to spread performance.
  • Spread tightening in Agency RMBS and asset-backed securities was a moderate contributor.

Economic and Market Review

  • U.S. economic growth found its footing after decelerating earlier this year, supported by a recovery in consumption and robust investment in artificial intelligence (AI). Reduced uncertainty and a rebound in financial markets helped consumption recover, particularly for high income households. AI-related investment has been a significant contributor to real gross domestic product (GDP) growth, even with official data likely understating the gains. Recent data looks relatively robust, prompting upward revisions to third-quarter GDP estimates.

  • That said, the labor market remains a key risk, as job creation has slowed from a three-month average of 176,000 in January 2024 to just 29,000 by August 2025, with gains narrowly concentrated in healthcare and government sectors. Slower immigration and increased retirements have tempered the impact of weaker job growth, resulting in just a modest rise in the unemployment rate to 4.3 percent in August. We expect it to continue rising gradually to around 4.5 percent by year-end, driven mainly by labor force entrants struggling to find jobs rather than widespread layoffs. Risks are skewed toward a sharper rise in unemployment, particularly as the conclusion of the government's deferred resignation program at the end of September could add up to 150,000 federal layoffs.

  • Looking ahead, we expect growth to stabilize at around 1.7 percent in 2025 and maintain a similar pace in 2026, supported by fiscal measures under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA), which includes retroactive tax cuts that should lift personal tax refunds and provisions to broaden business investment. AI optimism and elevated asset prices are likely to sustain high-income household consumption, though the economy remains bifurcated. Housing and lower income households continue to face challenges, with depressed home sales, subdued housing investment, and rising credit card and student loan delinquencies.

  • Inflation is expected to remain above target through early 2026, driven by tariff passthrough effects, before resuming a disinflationary trajectory. While actual tariff collections have lagged tariff rates due to delayed implementation and corporate mitigation measures, the administration is closing loopholes. We expect continued gradual tariff pass-through into consumer prices but see these as one-time price adjustments rather than persistent inflation.
  • The Federal Reserve (Fed) is back in easing mode, implementing a "risk management" rate cut in September. While the prospect of above-target inflation remains a risk, Fed policymakers largely expect slower growth to weigh on inflation, and tariff pass-through to be temporary. Meanwhile, slightly higher unemployment has tilted risks to its dual mandate toward a softer labor market. We see the September rate cut as part of a recalibration, with one to two more cuts this year, and easing to neutral over the course of 2026.

The referenced index is unmanaged and not available for direct investment. Index performance does not reflect transaction costs, fees, or expenses. Index data source: FundStation.

Bps (basis points): One basis point is equal to 0.01%. Carry: The difference between the cost of financing an asset and the interest received on that asset. Dry powder: highly liquid assets, such as cash or money market instruments, that can be invested when more attractive investment opportunities arise. Duration: A measure of the sensitivity of the price of a bond to a change in interest rates. In general, the higher the duration the more the bond’s price will change with interest rate movements. Spread: Spread refers to the difference between yields on different debt instruments and is used to understand the relative value and risks of different debt instruments.

Risk Considerations. This fund may not be suitable for all investors. In general, the value of a fixed-income security falls when interest rates rise and rises when interest rates fall. Longer term bonds are more sensitive to interest rate changes and subject to greater volatility than those with shorter maturities. During periods of declining rates, the interest rates on floating rate securities generally reset downward and their value is unlikely to rise to the same extent as comparable fixed rate securities. Investors in asset-backed securities, including collateralized loan obligations (“CLOs”), generally receive payments that are part interest and part return of principal. These payments may vary based on the rate loans are repaid. Some asset-backed securities may have structures that make their reaction to interest rates and other factors difficult to predict, making their prices volatile and they are subject to liquidity and valuation risk. High yield and unrated debt securities are at a greater risk of default than investment grade bonds and may be less liquid, which may increase volatility. The intrinsic value of the underlying stocks in which the fund invests may never be realized or the stock may decline in value. When market conditions are deemed appropriate, the fund may leverage to the full extent permitted by its investment policies and restrictions and applicable law. Leveraging will exaggerate the effect on net asset value of any increase or decrease in the market value of the fund’s portfolio. The use of short selling involves increased risks and costs. You risk paying more for a security than you received from its sale. Theoretically, stocks sold short have the risk of unlimited losses. The fund may invest in derivative instruments, which may be more volatile and less liquid, increasing the risk of loss when compared to traditional securities. Certain of the derivative instruments are also subject to the risks of counterparty default and adverse tax treatment. Instruments and strategies (such as borrowing transactions and reverse repurchase agreements) may provide leveraged exposure to a particular investment, which will magnify any gains or losses on those investments. Investments in reverse repurchase agreements expose the fund to the many of the same risks as investments in derivatives. The fund’s investments in other investment vehicles subject the fund to those risks and expenses affecting the investment vehicle. The fund’s investments in foreign securities carry additional risks when compared to U.S. securities, due to the impact of diplomatic, political, or economic developments in the country in question (investments in emerging markets securities are generally subject to an even greater level of risks). Investments in syndicated bank loans generally offer a floating interest rate and involve special types of risks. A highly liquid secondary market may not exist for the commodity-linked structured notes the fund invests in, and there can be no assurance that a highly liquid secondary market will develop. The fund’s exposure to the commodity markets may subject the fund to greater volatility as commodity-linked investments may be affected by changes in overall market movements, commodity data.title: Economic Reacceleration: A Contrarian View
data.description:
Over the past two weeks, we’ve addressed a persistent question: if the data signals weakness, why hasn’t the recession arrived? In “Slowdown Signals: Are Leading Indicators Flashing Red?” we examined the cracks forming beneath the economy’s surface. From deteriorating leading indicators to credit stress and cooling employment metrics, the evidence supported a cautious stance. In the follow-up, “Promised Recession... So Where Is It?”, we explored the tension between these bearish signals and the market’s resilience. Economic risk has not disappeared, but the timeline for a downturn has stretched further than most expected.

Both articles' tone was analytical but precise: the economy has not escaped danger. Leading economic indicators flash warning signs, from the Conference Board’s LEI to the ISM Manufacturing Index. Credit conditions remain tight with delinquencies on the rise, and employment trends, while stable on the surface, have started to weaken in rate-sensitive areas.

The consumer, long the backbone of the post-pandemic recovery, shows signs of fatigue. Excess savings have dwindled, credit card usage is climbing, and real wage gains have slowed. These are not signals of strength, but of late-cycle fragility.

Yet, despite these risks, the economy has not broken. The recession expected by most economists, strategists, and market commentators has yet to arrive. The S&P 500 continues to grind higher. Volatility remains muted. Consumer spending, while uneven, has not collapsed. And corporate earnings, though pressured in some sectors, have not cratered.

This divergence between bearish data and market behavior raises an important question: could the consensus be wrong? More specifically, what if the economy is not headed for contraction but toward a renewed phase of expansion? That’s the contrarian view, an economic reacceleration, and it’s worth considering, not because it is guaranteed, but because few are prepared for it. In markets, surprises matter more than forecasts. If the surprise is upside growth, the implications for asset prices, portfolio strategy, and risk management could be substantial.

Let’s dig into it.

The Case for Economic Reacceleration

While consensus remains cautious, there is a case, however tenuous, for economic reacceleration. This isn’t about ignoring risks. It’s about acknowledging that conditions aligning could drive a shift from stagnation to renewed growth.

First, financial conditions have eased substantially since late last year. Despite the Fed maintaining higher policy rates, markets have pushed bond yields lower and credit spreads tighter. Equities have rallied, creating a wealth effect that supports consumption, at least within the top 10% of the population that owns 87% of the equity market. Furthermore, the top 40% of income earners currently account for 60% of total consumption.

Adding to that backdrop, the Chicago Fed National Financial Conditions Index has turned increasingly accommodative, suggesting that monetary policy’s grip on the economy is slipping. This eases the burden on consumers and businesses, setting the stage for renewed activity.

Third, fiscal support remains more robust than many acknowledge. Federal spending continues at a high level, with infrastructure outlays and industrial policy subsidies supporting investment in energy, manufacturing, and technology. These initiatives feed directly into corporate capital expenditures. The AI investment boom is a clear example of spending accelerating not just in "big tech," but across the industrial supply chain. UBS has flagged this emerging capex cycle as a significant tailwind for mid-cycle expansion, which could offset a slowdown in consumer spending.

Fourth, labor market dynamics are more stable than the headlines suggest. While job openings have declined, layoffs remain low. Real disposable income has started to recover, supported by moderating inflation. This income stability allows consumers to maintain spending despite rising debt levels. Goldman Sachs has emphasized that consumption trends, particularly in services, remain strong enough to support growth in the near term. In the economically weighted ISM surveys, services (70% of the economy) are keeping the composite index in expansion territory.

Finally, there is the issue of data distortion. In recent months, significant revisions have been made to payrolls and GDP. Preliminary reports painted a picture of sharp deceleration, only for final data to show much more resilience. Goldman has warned that headline data may be underestimating actual activity. If that’s true, the economy may not need to rebound; it may already be stronger than it appears. We also see improvement in the Economic Composite Index, which comprises more than 100 data points, including manufacturing, services, leading indicators, and other economic factors.

Combined, these factors do not guarantee a reacceleration but offer a plausible case. They suggest the economy may be more resilient, supported, and responsive to easing conditions than the bearish narrative implies.

Supporting the Bull Market: Earnings and Valuation

If reacceleration gains traction, it becomes a fundamental driver of earnings growth. That’s the critical link. The equity rally has run ahead of fundamentals in some sectors. Valuations, particularly in technology and discretionary names, are stretched. Without earnings growth, they cannot be sustained. However, an economic reacceleration changes that dynamic. A pickup in nominal GDP would lift revenues. Stabilizing costs and improving operating leverage could support margins. As top-line growth returns, analysts would raise forward earnings estimates.

As UBS notes, they expect stronger earnings growth going into 2026, supported by stronger economic growth.

This path is especially relevant given the current market structure. Much of this year’s performance has been driven by a handful of mega-cap names; therefore, a broader earnings recovery, driven by an economic reacceleration, would allow the rally to widen. This is the theory, at least, and as breadth improves, volatility drops, and price-to-earnings multiples become more sustainable.

However, as we have noted previously, we had booming economic growth in 2020 and 2021, but the earnings growth remained confined to the largest companies. As shown in the chart above, earnings growth for the 493 remained weak. However, the expectation is that an improvement in economic growth, but not strong growth, will somehow allow the bottom 493 stocks to generate earnings growth as the Capex cycle for artificial intelligence engages. Maybe that is the case, but it remains a risk to investors. Nonetheless, the hope is that the economic reacceleration thesis, not a call for explosive expansion, will allow for durability in the current cycle, allowing the earnings tailwind to extend the bull market further than most expect.

Final Thoughts

For us, risk management remains essential. The case for economic reacceleration is certainly plausible, but it is also based on much "hope." Hope that consumers will continue to consume at a strong pace, that AI Capex will offset declining savings risk, and that earnings will continue to sustain themselves well above long-term growth trends indefinitely to support current valuations. That seems to be a significant risk.

While there is certainly a risk that “hopes” are disappointed, as investors, we must consider the possibility that an economic reacceleration comes to fruition. Such could leave overly defensive investors underexposed to equities, creating significant opportunity costs. Currently, many investors remain positioned for stagnation or slow deterioration. While that positioning aligns with the data, the yield curve, and historical patterns, the markets rarely reward consensus thinking. If reacceleration unfolds, it will catch many investors with an underweighted risk exposure.

Positioning for this shift does not require full conviction. But it does require readiness. Watch leading indicators for signs of stabilization. Monitor earnings revisions and forward guidance. Track credit spreads and yield curve behavior. These are the tells.

The opportunity lies in being early but not reckless. If growth is turning, earnings will follow, and earnings ultimately sustain bull markets.

Just something to consider.
